response_text:
:::writing
Every weekday morning, the same scene unfolds along our busiest urban corridors: buses full of people inch forward in the same gridlock as single-occupancy cars. Dozens of riders sit trapped behind a handful of drivers. It’s an inefficient use of scarce road space, and it’s one of the clearest signs that our transportation priorities need to change.

Dedicated bus lanes offer a straightforward fix.

A single bus can carry 40 to 60 passengers, sometimes far more during peak hours. Yet without dedicated lanes, those riders are forced to compete with private vehicles that often carry just one person. The result is a system where the most space‑efficient mode of road transport is slowed down by the least efficient one. Dedicated bus lanes flip that logic. They reward higher-capacity transportation by allowing buses to move reliably and quickly.

Reliability is not a minor benefit—it is the heart of effective transit. When buses are stuck in traffic, schedules fall apart. Riders miss connections, commutes become unpredictable, and public transit becomes a last resort rather than a first choice. Dedicated lanes dramatically improve travel time consistency. Even when traffic is heavy, riders know the bus will keep moving.

Critics often argue that dedicating a lane to buses will worsen congestion for drivers. In the short term, lane reallocations can feel disruptive. But cities that have implemented bus lanes—from New York to Bogotá—have repeatedly shown that moving more people in fewer vehicles reduces overall congestion. When buses become faster and more dependable, more commuters choose them over driving. Each full bus can remove dozens of cars from the road.

There are economic benefits as well. Faster buses reduce operating costs because the same vehicles can complete routes more quickly. That means transit agencies can run more frequent service without dramatically increasing budgets. Businesses along the corridor benefit too: improved transit access brings more foot traffic and makes it easier for employees and customers to reach them.

Perhaps most importantly, dedicated bus lanes make cities more equitable. Not everyone owns a car, and many of the people who rely on buses are students, seniors, and working families. When buses crawl through traffic, it is these riders who pay the price in lost time and opportunity. Prioritizing buses is, in effect, prioritizing the mobility of thousands of residents who depend on public transportation every day.

Urban streets are among the most valuable public spaces we have. The question is not whether they should move vehicles, but how many people they should move. Dedicated bus lanes
